children.name: Larus
children.description: Large gulls — the dominant seabirds of northern European coastlines and the most familiar waterbirds in Swedish cities. Gulls are supremely opportunistic generalists: they eat fish, invertebrates, carrion, garbage, and other birds' eggs and chicks with equal facility. Their intelligence is remarkable — herring gulls have been documented dropping shellfish onto hard surfaces to break them open, and individuals can recognise specific human faces. Stockholm's rocky archipelago supports large colonies of herring gulls and lesser black-backed gulls, whose calls are the ambient soundtrack of the Swedish coast. 'Gull identification' has become one of birding's most obsessive niches, with hybrids, unusual ages, and rare visitors drawing dedicated specialists.

children.name: Larus argentatus
children.description: The gråtrut — Sweden's largest breeding gull and the definitive 'seagull' of northern Europe. Nests on Swedish skerries, rocky islets, and increasingly on urban rooftops. Takes 4 years to reach adult plumage, passing through complex immature stages that challenge even expert identification.

children.name: Larus fuscus
children.description: The silltrut — similar in size to the herring gull but with dark grey-black wings. Breeds on Swedish coastal islands and archipelago skerries. A long-distance migrant, wintering in West Africa; has recently established wintering populations in Britain. Often seen alongside herring gulls at refuse sites.

children.name: Larus canus
children.description: The fiskmås — the most elegant of the common Swedish gulls, with a neat yellow-green bill and gentle expression. More inland than herring gulls; nests on lake islands throughout Sweden. Large numbers move through coastal Sweden in autumn. Its call is a plaintive, mewing cry quite unlike the raucous herring gull.

children.name: Larus hyperboreus
children.description: An Arctic gull — white-winged with no black in the plumage at any age. Visits Swedish Baltic coasts and harbours in winter. A scavenger of seal carcasses and seabird colonies in the Arctic. A sought-after sighting at Swedish winter harbours and fish docks.

children.name: Larus marinus
children.description: The world's largest gull, a formidable predator that takes seabirds, fish, and carrion; breeds on North Atlantic coasts and increasingly inland.

children.name: Larus glaucoides
children.description: A pale, white-winged Arctic gull breeding in Greenland and Canada; a sought-after winter visitor to North Atlantic harbours.

children.name: Larus delawarensis
children.description: An abundant North American gull named for the black ring on its yellow bill; common in parking lots, fields, and lakeshores.

children.name: Larus michahellis
children.description: A large Mediterranean gull with bright yellow legs, expanding northward into central Europe; increasingly urban-nesting.

children.name: Larus californicus
children.description: A medium-large western North American gull famed for saving Mormon settlers' crops from a locust plague in 1848; the state bird of Utah.

children.name: Larus occidentalis
children.description: A dark-backed gull of the Pacific coast of North America, often seen at seabird colonies where it preys on eggs and chicks.

children.name: Larus armenicus
children.description: A large white-headed gull breeding on high-altitude lakes in the Armenian Highlands and Turkey, including Lake Sevan. Closely related to the Caspian gull; its taxonomy is debated, with some authorities treating it as a subspecies. Winters along the coasts of the eastern Mediterranean and Red Sea.

children.name: Larus brachyrhynchus
children.description: Formerly treated as a subspecies of the common gull, this small-billed Pacific coast gull breeds in boreal Alaska and northwest Canada. It winters along the Pacific coast from Alaska south to California. Its gentle expression and small bill distinguish it from the larger ring-billed gull.

children.name: Larus cachinnans
children.description: A large, long-winged white-headed gull breeding around the Caspian, Black, and Aral Seas, now spreading west into central Europe. Identified by its long sloping forehead, pale eye, and long parallel-sided bill with a small gonys spot. Increasingly seen at western European rubbish tips and harbours in winter.

children.name: Larus crassirostris
children.description: A medium-large gull of East Asian coasts, recognised by its yellow bill with a black band before the red tip. Breeds in large, dense colonies on rocky islands off Japan, Korea, and China. A common sight at East Asian fishing harbours; the species' mewing calls inspired the Japanese haiku tradition of 'umineko' (sea cat).

children.name: Larus dominicanus
children.description: The dominant large gull of the Southern Hemisphere, replacing the great black-backed gull in the south. Known to attack the backs of living southern right whales off Argentina's Valdés Peninsula to feed on skin and blubber, a behaviour that has increased dramatically in recent decades. Breeds from Antarctica to New Zealand and South Africa.

children.name: Larus glaucescens
children.description: The common large gull of the north Pacific coast, with pale grey wingtips rather than the black wingtips of most large gulls. Breeds from Alaska to Washington State and winters south to Baja California. Forms hybrids with western, herring, and glaucous gulls wherever ranges overlap.

children.name: Larus heermanni
children.description: A strikingly dark grey gull of the Pacific coast of North America with a red bill and white head in breeding plumage. Almost the entire world population breeds on Isla Rasa in the Gulf of California, Mexico, making it highly vulnerable to habitat disturbance. Kleptoparasitises pelicans and other seabirds after they surface with fish.

children.name: Larus livens
children.description: A large, dark-backed gull endemic to the Gulf of California, closely related to the western gull and distinguished by its vivid yellow feet. Almost all breeding occurs on a handful of islands in the Gulf; numbers are small. Feeds heavily on fish and marine invertebrates in the warm, productive waters of the gulf.

children.name: Larus pacificus
children.description: A large, massively billed gull endemic to southern Australia, with the heaviest bill of any gull relative to body size — used to smash open shells. Common on southern Australian beaches, headlands, and offshore islands. Adults take four or more years to develop their white head and clean black mantle.

children.name: Larus schistisagus
children.description: A large, dark-mantled gull of the North Pacific breeding along the coasts of Russia, Japan, and the Bering Sea. Its dark slate-grey back contrasts with a distinctive 'string of pearls' pattern on the wingtip. A rare but sought-after vagrant on the Pacific coast of North America.

children.name: Larus smithsonianus
children.description: The most abundant large gull of eastern North America, previously considered a subspecies of the Eurasian herring gull but now recognised as a distinct species. Breeds from the Great Lakes and Atlantic coast north to the Canadian Arctic. A dominant scavenger and predator at fish docks, landfills, and coastal colonies.

children.name: Larus thayeri
children.description: A medium-large gull breeding in the high Arctic of Canada with a complex taxonomic history, treated variously as a full species, subspecies of Iceland gull, or herring gull race. Its wingtip pattern is intermediate — darker than Iceland gull but paler than herring gull. Winters along the Pacific coast south to California.

children.name: Larus vegae
children.description: A large white-headed gull breeding across Arctic Siberia and wintering along East Asian coasts and the Pacific coast of North America. Closely related to the American herring gull; previously treated as conspecific. Named after the Swedish exploration vessel Vega, which wintered near its type locality.

children.name: Larus belcheri
children.description: A striking large gull of Peru and Chile's Pacific coast with a yellow bill banded in black and red and a distinctive black tail band. Common in harbours and on beaches of the cold Humboldt Current system, where it competes with boobies and pelicans for fish. Named after naval officer and Pacific explorer Sir Edward Belcher.

children.name: Larus atlanticus
children.description: A large, handsome gull of the South Atlantic coast of Argentina, Uruguay, and southern Brazil with a yellow bill banded in black and red. Closely resembles the band-tailed gull of the Pacific coast; the two were formerly treated as conspecific. Listed as Near Threatened due to its restricted breeding range on a handful of Argentine coastal islands.

children.name: Larus heuglini
children.description: A large, dark-mantled gull breeding in Arctic Russia from the Kola Peninsula to the Taimyr, wintering along the coasts of the Red Sea, Indian Ocean, and East Africa. Its dark grey mantle and pink legs separate it from the herring gull; it has long been controversial whether it constitutes a full species or a subspecies. Named after German naturalist Martin Theodor von Heuglin.

children.name: Larus barabensis
children.description: A medium-large gull of the steppe lakes of Kazakhstan and western Siberia, recently recognised as distinct from the Caspian gull. Breeding on inland saline lakes of the Baraba steppe, it winters along the coasts of the Arabian Sea and Indian subcontinent. Its taxonomy within the 'herring gull complex' remains debated, but genetic studies support its species status.

children.name: Larus naumanni
children.description: A large, pale-mantled gull of Mongolian and Chinese plateau lakes, closely allied to the yellow-legged gull complex. Breeds on high-altitude inland lakes across Mongolia and adjacent China, and winters along the coasts of eastern China and Korea. Only recently widely accepted as a full species separate from the Caspian gull, following genetic analysis.

children.name: Chroicocephalus
children.description: Hooded and black-headed gulls — smaller than Larus, characterised in summer by dark hoods that range from black to chocolate brown. The black-headed gull (skrattmås) is the most numerous gull in Stockholm and across Sweden's inland waters: its breeding colonies produce a cacophony of laughing calls that give it its Swedish name ('skratt' = laugh). Despite the name, the summer hood is actually dark chocolate brown, not black. Black-headed gulls are inland birds as much as coastal ones, following ploughs, roosting on city parks, and commuting between feeding areas and inland reservoirs in spectacular evening flights.

children.name: Chroicocephalus ridibundus
children.description: The skrattmås — Stockholm's most numerous gull and the most common inland gull in Sweden. Breeds in large, noisy colonies on marsh islands and lake shores. White leading edge to wing is distinctive in flight. Loses its dark hood in winter, leaving just a dark ear spot.

children.name: Chroicocephalus minutus
children.description: The world's smallest gull at just 28 cm. Black underwing and black hood in summer. Breeds in Scandinavian and Baltic marshes. Occurs regularly on passage along Swedish coasts. Has a delicate, tern-like flight, dipping to the surface to pick off insects.

children.name: Chroicocephalus philadelphia
children.description: A small, tern-like gull breeding in boreal Canada, uniquely nesting in spruce trees; winters on North American coasts.

children.name: Chroicocephalus novaehollandiae
children.description: The ubiquitous small gull of Australian beaches, parks, and car parks; bold and highly successful around humans.

children.name: Chroicocephalus cirrocephalus
children.description: A pale grey-hooded gull of African and South American wetlands and coasts; adaptable and locally common.

children.name: Chroicocephalus brunnicephalus
children.description: A medium-sized hooded gull breeding on high-altitude lakes across central Asia, including the Tibetan Plateau and Himalayas. Its pale brown hood is less contrasting than the black-headed gull's chocolate hood. Winters on the coasts and large rivers of the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Chroicocephalus bulleri
children.description: An endemic gull of New Zealand, unique among the world's gulls for its entirely black bill in adults. Breeds inland on braided riverbeds of South Island, making it highly vulnerable to introduced predators and river management. Classified as Endangered due to rapid population decline.

children.name: Chroicocephalus genei
children.description: A distinctive, elongated gull of the Mediterranean and Middle East with a long drooping bill giving it a unique profile. Lacks a hood at all seasons, instead showing a pale pinkish flush to the white head. Breeds colonially in coastal lagoons and saltpans from the Iberian Peninsula to Kazakhstan.

children.name: Chroicocephalus maculipennis
children.description: The most common small gull of South America's southern cone, with a rich chocolate-brown hood in summer and white head with ear-spot in winter. Breeds along coasts and on inland lakes from central Chile and Argentina south to Tierra del Fuego. Often abundant in harbours and fishing villages.

children.name: Chroicocephalus serranus
children.description: A black-hooded gull breeding at extremely high altitudes — up to 4,600 m — on Andean lakes and wetlands in Peru, Bolivia, and Chile. The only gull that breeds predominantly above the tree line in the high Andes. Descends to Pacific coastal areas outside the breeding season.

children.name: Sterna
children.description: Sea terns — elegant, sharply pointed seabirds built for plunge-diving into the sea for small fish. Terns are the migrants par excellence: the Arctic tern (silvertärna) makes the longest migration of any animal on Earth, breeding in the Arctic (including northern Sweden's coast) and wintering in the Antarctic, covering 70,000–90,000 km annually — it experiences more daylight than any other creature. Common terns (fisktärna) breed in noisy, fiercely defensive colonies on Swedish coastal rocks and lake islands. Terns dive at speed into the water to catch fish, folding their wings at the last moment. Their aggressive colony defence — dive-bombing and occasionally striking intruders — makes tern colonies an exciting experience for visiting naturalists.

children.name: Sterna hirundo
children.description: The fisktärna — Sweden's most familiar tern, breeding on coastal skerries and lake islands throughout the country. Easily confused with Arctic tern; best separated by its longer legs, orange-red bill with black tip, and tail length. Aggressively defends colonies, drawing blood from human intruders.

children.name: Sterna paradisaea
children.description: The silvertärna — holder of the world's longest migration record: up to 90,000 km annually from Arctic to Antarctic and back. Breeding birds on Swedish coasts experience near-continuous daylight in both hemispheres. Virtually all-red bill (no black tip), shorter legs, and longer tail streamers than common tern. Attacks intruders fearlessly, sometimes drawing blood from the scalp.

children.name: Sterna sandvicensis
children.description: The largest regular Swedish tern. Distinctive black bill with yellow tip. Breeds in colonies on Swedish west coast islands and Baltic skerries. Has a harsh, rasping call. Winters along African coasts. A good fish-finder — other seabirds follow Sandwich terns to fish shoals.

children.name: Sterna dougallii
children.description: A graceful pale tern with a rosy breast flush in spring; globally scarce and the focus of intensive conservation in the North Atlantic.

children.name: Sterna forsteri
children.description: A North American marsh-breeding tern very similar to the common tern; distinguished by silvery primaries and a winter eye-mask.

children.name: Sterna vittata
children.description: A southern-hemisphere tern breeding on subantarctic islands and the Antarctic coast; replaces the Arctic tern in the far south.

children.name: Sterna acuticauda
children.description: A scarce freshwater tern of South and Southeast Asian river systems, unique among typical terns for its black belly in breeding plumage. Breeds on large sandy river islands on the Ganges, Brahmaputra, and Irrawaddy systems. Declining severely due to river sand-mining, damming, and human disturbance; listed as Endangered.

children.name: Sterna aurantia
children.description: A richly coloured freshwater tern of South Asian rivers with a bright orange-yellow bill and deeply forked tail. Breeds on exposed sandbanks and rocky islets in major river systems from Pakistan to Thailand. A resident species unlike most terns; rarely ventures to the coast.

children.name: Sterna repressa
children.description: A medium-sized tern of the northwestern Indian Ocean breeding on islands in the Red Sea, Arabian Sea, and Persian Gulf. Its grey underparts and white cheeks make it one of the darkest typical terns outside breeding season. Forages in offshore waters for small fish.

children.name: Sterna striata
children.description: New Zealand's most common tern, breeding throughout both main islands and sub-Antarctic islands. A medium-sized white tern with a black cap, white forehead, and black bill. Often forms large pre-migratory flocks in late summer before crossing the Tasman Sea to winter in southeastern Australia.

children.name: Sterna sumatrana
children.description: A graceful tropical tern of the Indo-Pacific, nearly all-white with a distinctive black nape band connecting the eyes and a light build. Breeds on coral islands and atolls from the Seychelles to Australia and Micronesia. Often seen hunting over shallow reef flats in small, fluttering parties.

children.name: Rissa
children.description: Kittiwakes — small, gentle, truly pelagic gulls that nest on sheer sea cliffs and spend the rest of the year far out at sea.

children.name: Rissa tridactyla
children.description: An oceanic gull nesting in dense, noisy colonies on vertical sea cliffs; populations have crashed in the North Atlantic due to declining sand-eel stocks; now Vulnerable.

children.name: Rissa brevirostris
children.description: A range-restricted kittiwake breeding almost entirely on a few Bering Sea islands; distinguished by short bill and bright red legs; considered Vulnerable.

children.name: Thalasseus
children.description: Crested terns — large terns with shaggy black crests and slender bills, plunge-diving for fish in warm and temperate seas.

children.name: Thalasseus maximus
children.description: A large orange-billed crested tern of American and West African coasts, nesting in dense colonies on sandy islands.

children.name: Thalasseus bergii
children.description: A widespread Indo-Pacific crested tern with a yellow bill, often following fishing boats and resting on jetties.

children.name: Thalasseus bengalensis
children.description: A medium-large crested tern of the Indian Ocean and western Pacific with a bright orange bill, smaller and slimmer than the greater crested tern. Breeds on islands from the Mediterranean to Australia; the single colony in Libya is the only breeding record for the western Mediterranean. Highly mobile, wintering from East Africa to Australia.

children.name: Thalasseus elegans
children.description: A slender, droopy-billed crested tern that nests almost entirely on Isla Rasa, Baja California, in one of the world's most concentrated seabird colonies. Distinguished from the royal tern by its longer, more curved orange-red bill and slimmer build. Winters along the Pacific coast of South America as far south as Chile.

children.name: Thalasseus acuflavidus
children.description: Recently split from the Sandwich tern, this Atlantic-American crested tern breeds from the southeastern US to the Caribbean and Argentina. It has the same black bill with yellow tip as the Sandwich tern but is genetically distinct and shows subtle structural differences. Nests in dense mixed colonies with other terns on sandy barrier islands.

children.name: Thalasseus sandvicensis
children.description: A large crested tern of European and western Asian coasts with a distinctive black bill tipped yellow, named after Sandwich in Kent where the type specimen was collected. Breeds in dense, noisy colonies on northern European coasts, often mixed with black-headed gulls for protection. Winters along the coasts of tropical West Africa.

children.name: Thalasseus zimmermanni
children.description: One of the world's rarest birds — fewer than 100 individuals are known to exist — breeding only on a handful of rocky islands off China and Taiwan. Distinguished from the greater crested tern by its yellow bill with a black tip and white-based black crest. Rediscovered breeding in 2000 after 63 years without confirmed records; listed as Critically Endangered.

children.name: Sternula
children.description: The smallest terns — fast-flying, yellow-billed species that hover and plunge into shallow water for tiny fish.

children.name: Sternula albifrons
children.description: A tiny tern with a yellow black-tipped bill and white forehead, nesting on bare shingle and sand; vulnerable to beach disturbance and rising seas.

children.name: Sternula antillarum
children.description: North America's smallest tern, nesting on river sandbars and beaches; some inland populations are endangered due to river management.

children.name: Sternula balaenarum
children.description: A tiny tern endemic to southern Africa with an entirely black bill distinguishing it from all other little terns. Breeds on the Skeleton Coast of Namibia and disperses north along the West African coast after breeding. One of the world's least-known terns; its small, range-restricted population makes it Near Threatened.

children.name: Sternula lorata
children.description: A rare small tern endemic to the hyperarid Atacama coast of Peru and Chile, one of the most threatened terns in the Americas. Nests on bare, pebbly desert ground very close to the high-tide line. Listed as Endangered, with total population estimated at fewer than 1,000 birds.

children.name: Sternula nereis
children.description: A small tern of Australian, New Caledonian, and New Zealand coastal waters, with an orange-yellow bill and pale grey upperparts. Nests on open sandy beaches where it is highly vulnerable to human disturbance, predators, and storm inundation. The New Zealand subspecies is listed as Threatened.

children.name: Sternula saundersi
children.description: A small tern of the northwestern Indian Ocean, breeding on islands in the Red Sea and along the Arabian Sea coast. Separated from the little tern by its greyer rump and different wing pattern; the two species overlap in range in the Middle East. Named after English ornithologist Howard Saunders.

children.name: Sternula sinensis
children.description: A critically endangered small tern breeding on a tiny number of sandy beaches in China, Taiwan, and the Philippines. The global population is estimated at just a few thousand birds and has been severely impacted by coastal development. Conservation efforts in Taiwan using artificial nesting platforms have produced encouraging results.

children.name: Sternula superciliaris
children.description: A small freshwater tern resident on the large river systems of tropical South America, from the Amazon and Orinoco to the Río de la Plata. Its vivid yellow bill and white supercilium stripe distinguish it from the ocean-going least tern. Nests on exposed sandbars exposed by falling river levels during the dry season.

children.name: Chlidonias
children.description: Marsh terns — dark-bodied freshwater terns that pick insects and small prey from the surface of marshes rather than plunge-diving.

children.name: Chlidonias niger
children.description: A dainty marsh tern, sooty black in summer, that dips to the water for insects over reedy lakes; declining with wetland drainage.

children.name: Chlidonias hybrida
children.description: A marsh tern of southern wetlands with grey underparts and white cheeks in summer, nesting on floating vegetation.

children.name: Chlidonias leucopterus
children.description: A strikingly black-and-white marsh tern in breeding plumage, breeding in eastern European and Asian wetlands and migrating to Africa and Australasia.

children.name: Chlidonias albostriatus
children.description: New Zealand's only endemic tern, breeding exclusively on the braided rivers of South Island's eastern plains. Recognised by its orange bill and legs contrasting with a black cap and pale grey body. Classified as Endangered due to introduced mammalian predators and degradation of braided river habitats.

children.name: Hydroprogne
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Caspian tern, the largest tern in the world.

children.name: Hydroprogne caspia
children.description: The world's largest tern, gull-sized with a massive blood-red bill; cosmopolitan but local, breeding on coasts and large lakes including the Baltic.

children.name: Gelochelidon
children.description: Gull-billed terns — stout, short-billed terns that hawk insects and small vertebrates over land as well as water.

children.name: Gelochelidon nilotica
children.description: A pale tern with a heavy black bill that, unusually, hunts insects, crabs, and lizards over fields and marshes rather than diving for fish.

children.name: Onychoprion
children.description: Tropical seabird terns with dark upperparts that spend years continuously at sea before returning to breed on remote islands.

children.name: Onychoprion fuscatus
children.description: An intensely pelagic tropical tern that can stay airborne over the ocean for years between breeding; nests in vast, deafening island colonies.

children.name: Onychoprion aleuticus
children.description: A scarce, poorly known tern breeding on coastal Alaska and far-eastern Russia, with a distinctive white forehead contrasting with its dark plumage. Winters in the western Pacific, where remarkably its wintering grounds remained unknown to science until the early 21st century. Declining in parts of Alaska for unknown reasons.

children.name: Onychoprion anaethetus
children.description: A widespread tropical tern with a distinctive white bridle stripe from bill to above the eye, separating the black cap from the dark brown back. Breeds on rocky and coral islands throughout tropical seas from the Caribbean to the Indian and Pacific Oceans. Slightly smaller and browner-backed than the sooty tern, which often shares the same colonies.

children.name: Onychoprion lunatus
children.description: A small, dark tropical tern of the central Pacific Ocean with a crescent-shaped white eyebrow giving it a spectacled appearance. Breeds on isolated oceanic islands including the Hawaiian chain, Johnston Atoll, and Polynesian atolls. Forages far out to sea, often in mixed flocks with other tropical terns and associated with tuna schools.

children.name: Rynchops
children.description: Skimmers — unique terns with a lower mandible longer than the upper, used to slice through the water surface in flight to snap up fish.

children.name: Rynchops niger
children.description: The only American skimmer, ploughing the water surface with its longer lower bill to catch fish by touch; the only bird with vertical-slit pupils.

children.name: Rynchops albicollis
children.description: A scarce skimmer of the major river systems of South and Southeast Asia, with a vivid orange-red bill tipped yellow and white neck collar. Breeds on exposed sandy riverbanks of the Ganges, Chambal, Irrawaddy, and Mekong. Listed as Vulnerable due to severe decline from sand-mining, fishing, and human disturbance at nesting beaches.

children.name: Rynchops flavirostris
children.description: The only skimmer of the African continent, breeding on sandbanks of major rivers including the Zambezi, Niger, and Congo. Its bright orange-yellow bill distinguishes it from the Indian skimmer. Forages by skimming calm river backwaters and lake shores at dawn and dusk, snapping up small fish by touch.

children.name: Ichthyaetus
children.description: Large dark-hooded gulls of southern Eurasia and Africa, several with striking black or grey hoods in breeding plumage.

children.name: Ichthyaetus melanocephalus
children.description: An elegant gull with a jet-black hood and blood-red bill, expanding rapidly north and west across Europe from a Mediterranean stronghold.

children.name: Ichthyaetus audouinii
children.description: A rare Mediterranean gull with a coral-red bill, once one of the world's scarcest gulls; recovered strongly thanks to colony protection.

children.name: Ichthyaetus hemprichii
children.description: A dark-backed, brown-hooded gull of the Red Sea, Persian Gulf, and Arabian Sea coastlines. Its chocolate-brown hood, dark mantle, and bicoloured bill of yellow tipped with orange and black make it distinctive. Breeds on rocky and coral islands and is the characteristic large gull of Middle Eastern harbours and fishing docks.

children.name: Ichthyaetus ichthyaetus
children.description: The largest of the hooded gulls — nearly as large as a herring gull — with a jet-black hood and yellow-orange bill marked with a black subterminal band. Breeds on inland lakes and steppe wetlands of central Asia, from the Caspian to Mongolia. Winters along the coasts of the Indian subcontinent, Arabian Sea, and East Africa.

children.name: Ichthyaetus leucophthalmus
children.description: A slender, dark-backed gull endemic to the Red Sea region with a striking black hood, red bill, and conspicuous white eye-crescent. Breeds almost exclusively on small islands in the Red Sea, making it one of the most range-restricted gulls in the world. The distinctive white crescents above and below the eye are visible even at long range.

children.name: Ichthyaetus relictus
children.description: A mysterious gull of the central Asian steppe, breeding on remote inland lakes in Mongolia, Russia, and northwestern China. Unknown to western science until 1929 and not fully described until 1971; its wintering range in the Yellow Sea was confirmed only in the 1990s. Listed as Vulnerable; the total population may be as low as 12,000 birds.

children.name: Leucophaeus
children.description: American hooded gulls including the abundant laughing gull and the desert-nesting grey gull.

children.name: Leucophaeus atricilla
children.description: A black-hooded American gull named for its raucous laughing call, common along Atlantic and Gulf coasts and a familiar beach scavenger.

children.name: Leucophaeus modestus
children.description: A uniquely desert-nesting gull that breeds in the rainless interior of the Atacama, commuting daily to feed on the Pacific coast.

children.name: Leucophaeus fuliginosus
children.description: The world's rarest gull, endemic to the Galápagos Islands with a total population of only around 400 individuals. Entirely dark sooty grey with a black hood and bright red orbital ring. A scavenger and kleptoparasite around the islands' sea lion colonies and fishing villages, it exploits a wide range of food sources.

children.name: Leucophaeus pipixcan
children.description: A small black-hooded gull that breeds in the prairies of central North America and makes one of the longest migrations of any gull, wintering on the Pacific coast of South America. Follows farm machinery to catch disturbed insects and earthworms — once in vast flocks of millions that have declined substantially in recent decades.

children.name: Leucophaeus scoresbii
children.description: A strikingly coloured gull of the coasts and islands of southern South America, with a deep red bill, red legs, and dark grey plumage in adults. Common in the Falkland Islands and around Tierra del Fuego, where it scavenges at penguin and sea lion colonies. Named after the Arctic explorer William Scoresby.

children.name: Xema
children.description: A monotypic genus for Sabine's gull, a small, tern-like Arctic gull with a boldly patterned wing.

children.name: Xema sabini
children.description: A small high-Arctic gull with a striking black, white, and grey wing triangle and a forked tail; winters at sea off Africa and South America.

children.name: Pagophila
children.description: A monotypic genus for the ivory gull, the only entirely white gull, tied to the high Arctic pack ice.

children.name: Pagophila eburnea
children.description: A ghostly all-white gull of the high Arctic that scavenges at polar bear kills and rarely leaves the pack ice; Near Threatened and declining with sea-ice loss.

children.name: Anous
children.description: Noddies — dark-plumaged tropical terns with distinctive pale or white caps, breeding on tropical islands across all three major oceans. Unlike most terns, noddies do not plunge-dive but snatch prey from the surface, and they often rest on the sea rather than staying constantly airborne.

children.name: Anous stolidus
children.description: The most abundant and widespread noddy, uniformly dark brown with a pale grey-white cap, breeding on tropical and subtropical islands throughout the Atlantic, Indian, and Pacific Oceans. Often perches in trees and shrubs rather than on bare ground. Sometimes follows schools of tuna or dolphins to feed on fish driven to the surface.

children.name: Anous minutus
children.description: A smaller, blacker version of the brown noddy with a sharper white cap, nesting in trees on tropical Pacific, Atlantic, and Indian Ocean islands. Often nests in the same colonies as brown noddies but occupies different vegetation. Its extremely high number of subspecies reflects the isolation of island populations across its vast range.

children.name: Anous tenuirostris
children.description: A small, slender-billed noddy of the central Indian Ocean, breeding in enormous colonies on Heron Island (Australia) and the Seychelles. Distinguished from the black noddy by a whiter, more extensive cap and more slender bill. Feeds by dipping to the sea surface, snatching zooplankton and small fish in calm tropical waters.

children.name: Procelsterna
children.description: Blue noddies — tiny, pale terns of the tropical Pacific, the most delicate members of the Laridae, feeding on zooplankton and tiny fish by dipping to the sea surface.

children.name: Procelsterna cerulea
children.description: An ethereally pale, almost translucent pale grey tern of the central and eastern tropical Pacific Ocean. One of the smallest members of the gull-tern family, it nests in cavities and crevices on low coral islands. Unlike darker noddies, its pale coloration makes it difficult to see against a grey ocean sky.

children.name: Procelsterna albivitta
children.description: A pale grey noddy of the subtropical western and central Pacific, breeding on Norfolk Island and Lord Howe Island in the Tasman Sea. Recently split from the blue noddy based on genetic and morphological differences. Nests on rocky cliff faces and forages over pelagic waters for small fish and invertebrates.

children.name: Gygis
children.description: White terns — ethereally beautiful, entirely snow-white tropical seabirds that famously lay their single egg on a bare branch with no nest. The large blue eye and translucent wings give them an otherworldly appearance.

children.name: Gygis alba
children.description: The famous fairy tern of tropical islands — entirely white with translucent wings and a deep blue eye, laying its single egg in a fork of a bare branch with no nesting material whatsoever. The city bird of Honolulu, where it nests on trees in urban parks. Found on tropical islands throughout the Pacific, Atlantic, and Indian Oceans.

children.name: Gygis microrhyncha
children.description: A smaller, slender-billed white tern restricted to the Marquesas and a few other remote Polynesian islands in the central Pacific. Recently split from the white tern based on its smaller size, finer bill, and genetic differentiation. Like its relative, it lays its single egg directly on a branch without building a nest.

children.name: Larosterna
children.description: A monotypic genus containing only the Inca tern, one of the most striking and unusual seabirds in the world, unique to the cold Humboldt Current system of the South American Pacific coast.

children.name: Larosterna inca
children.description: Arguably the most flamboyant seabird in the world: dark slate-grey with a bright red bill, red legs, white moustache plumes, and a yellow gape wattle. Endemic to the cold, fish-rich Humboldt Current of Peru and Chile, where it nests in cliff crevices and guano-rich sea caves. Highly dependent on anchoveta; populations suffer during El Niño events when fish become scarce.
